The Illusion Of Separateness

[Rasa World](https://rasajournal.substack.com/s/rasa-world/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# The Illusion Of Separateness

### Why it is damaging to think of ourselves as being unique in our experiences of life and how overcoming this conceit leads us to something much greater within ourselves.

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[Garima Garg](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

Jan 22, 2026

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lfn5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9c2ec84e-913c-43aa-a8df-4d1848c66852_1698x2554.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lfn5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9c2ec84e-913c-43aa-a8df-4d1848c66852_1698x2554.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*Adi Shankaracharya’s maternal birthplace, now known as Adi Shankara Nilayam, at the Chinmaya International Foundation in Veliyanad, Ernakulam.*

Earlier this month, I was at the maternal birthplace of Adi Shankaracharya in Ernakulam, Kerala. Legend has it that he was only seven years old when he wanted to leave his home and become a monk. When his mother did not allow, fate apparently intervened and had a crocodile bite the young Shankara while he was bathing in the traditional family pond. At this, his mother became disheartened and felt it was some sort of a warning sign to her from the divine itself— *let your son go if you want him to survive*. So she did and so he went off around the country, seeking and sharing his knowledge. He is believed to have lived around 8th century and died at the young age of 32. But despite his short life, he resurrected Hinduism from its philosophical obscurities and instilled a depth of devotion in its common practice. He is revered even today because he managed to do the impossible— creating a bridge between the high thought of an intellectual and unshakeable faith of a believer.

He is now regarded as the most prominent exponent of Advaita Vedanta, i.e., the philosophy of non-duality. If the ultimate reality or the existence itself is Brahman, then the part of it that manifests within us is Atman. In other words, *tat-tvam-asi*, you are that which is manifest in the entire existence. There are quite a few family temples around the house where the monk was born and one of them is a self-manifesting Ayyappa temple, which bears the mantra *tat-tvam-asi* at its entrance, much like the Sabarimala temple of the deity.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!abrc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb6dfe142-eb3f-42ba-91ec-3f1b32d56e06_5712x4284.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!abrc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb6dfe142-eb3f-42ba-91ec-3f1b32d56e06_5712x4284.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*Chinmaya International Foundation has preserved the original Kerala architecture of Adi Shankara’s maternal grandparents house.*

But the problem with high thought is that it can be difficult to *get* it. What does it even mean to express that each one of us, including animals and plants, are the manifest divine itself? And more importantly, does it really matter beyond scholarly debates? In other words, what does it mean in the “real” world of hierarchies where privileges are doled out as per one’s economic status, influence, and capabilities?

Perhaps, one way to approach this is through arts. At the Nilayam, I was attending a residential workshop about Navarasa, i.e., the nine emotions of shringara (beauty), hasya (laughter), raudra (anger), veera (courage), adbhuta (wonder), vibhatsa (disgust), karuna (compassion), bhayanaka (fear), and shanta (peace). The workshop was designed to explain the theory of Navarasa and its application to different art forms such as painting, music, dance, literature, and even modern commercial cinema in India. While watching and listening to the artists, the words of Sri Lankan writer Ananda Coomaraswamy rang in my mind. In his book, Dance of Siva, he expounds the rasa theory and makes the point that the rasika, i.e., the audience, is just as important as the rasa, the art, and the artist.

When you think about it, you realise it is not mere theory but a fact of life itself. As humans, we have been creating art since we lived in caves. We have done so not out of vanity or boredom but to express our experiences, our lives, and our realities. True art, therefore, is not distinguished by its sophistication or nuance but by its universality. Yet, in the Hindu philosophical worldview, even the universally-appealing art is meant to elevate us as humans rather than keep us where we are. What struck me most deeply about these artists in Kerala was the devotion with which they practiced their craft. While devotion does not dissolve ego, it does quieten it every now and then. And when that happens, the magic of being a human who is striving to merge with the larger divine comes alive. It is literally Advaita Vedanta in motion.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ntBL!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F31615da0-4448-413d-99c7-05e5f717a285_2462x3283.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ntBL!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F31615da0-4448-413d-99c7-05e5f717a285_2462x3283.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zIB0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F57354669-0b57-44e5-bf08-ec43755af59e_3225x4956.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zIB0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F57354669-0b57-44e5-bf08-ec43755af59e_3225x4956.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*More glimpses of the Chinmaya International Foundation campus that houses the Adi Shankara Nilayam.*

As I sat there, I realised how much of a far cry this perspective on art and in fact, life itself, was from the modern worldview where art either belongs to the genius or the porn-addled mind. In this dichotomy, masses remain perpetually lost for insight, beauty, and expression. Ultimately, I realised this was not just a problem with modern art but modernity itself.

A civilisation that has “I think, therefore I am” at its heart cannot come to terms with the idea of an existence without a self-oriented thinking. While today we bemoan “narcissists”, such people are the logical conclusion of a society that defines the self as everything *it* creates or experiences. It is this internalised mode of thinking that creates an illusion of separateness within us and in doing so, severs our relationship with everything beyond our thinking mind. It does not take much imagination to see why that greatly limits our perception and experience of reality itself.

In other words, the illusion of separateness that we carry as modern individuals is perhaps our greatest tragedy despite our material abundance. We carve ourselves out of the crowd to stand out, to be unique, to be special. Yet, all we end up accomplishing in this conceit is an increasingly greater and deeper sense of our insignificance. We carry on, continuing this vicious circle, unable to break out of it because the mere idea of letting go of the self feels catastrophic to us. It feels like death when it is, in fact, our true birth as a human being.
